caveats: a: Agricultural inspectors are only one occupation within a broader administrative code.
a: Private-sector inspectors in BLS data do not share identical government workflows.
rho: Automation of administrative preparation does not authorize autonomous enforcement or final grading decisions.
e: Private contractors may support agencies but are not establishments inside this public-administration code.
s5: Agency reorganizations, intergovernmental transfers, outsourcing awards, and elected-administration changes are not company control sales.
q: Agency budget retention and fee-setting rules vary but are not commercial benefit retention.
d5: Farm cash receipts are an economic-activity proxy, not a regulatory-output measure.
d5: One-year forecasts do not capture policy discontinuities.
o: Reduced workload belongs in d5; internal automation does not remove the accountable regulator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Tax-, fee-, or appropriation-funded public administration serving producers, consumers, markets, and other agencies rather than commercial external customers; no coherent lower-middle-market private-firm revenue model exists within this NAICS code.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default outsourced private-firm lens is inapplicable because Census defines 926140 as government establishments. The statutory population is retained and acquisition-transfer and commercial-retention constructs are marked missing rather than importing private vendors from other industries.
lens.excluded_activities: Private laboratories, certifiers, consultants, cooperatives, commodity brokers, farm operators, food manufacturers, captive commercial compliance teams, and vendors classified outside public administration.
lens.included_activities: Government establishments planning, administering, and coordinating agricultural production, marketing, and utilization programs, including education, promotion, commodity grading, inspection, quarantine, pest and weed regulation, and enforcement.

a.bridge: BLS reports 12,660 agricultural inspectors nationally, including 4,880 in state government, 2,810 in combined government, and 890 in local government, and defines their work as inspecting commodities, equipment, facilities, fish, and logging operations for health, quality, safety, and legal compliance. I translate this occupation evidence plus the code's planning and administration scope into a substantial but nonmajority wage-weighted AI-exposed share covering document review, application processing, report drafting, risk targeting, image analysis, scheduling, guidance, and data reconciliation. It may overstate substitution because legal findings require field evidence and accountable judgment, or understate exposure in program-administration roles.
caveats: Agricultural inspectors are only one occupation within a broader administrative code.
Private-sector inspectors in BLS data do not share identical government workflows.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit wage-weighted occupation and time-use data, plus agency pilots reporting accuracy, appeal rates, inspection yield, human review, cycle-time, and labor-hour effects.
a.rationale: AI can accelerate records, analytics, triage, and standardized grading support, while sampling, facility visits, enforcement, stakeholder engagement, and final regulatory accountability remain human.

d5.bridge: USDA forecasts 2026 farm cash receipts of $514.7 billion, down 4.5% in real terms from 2025, while crop receipts decline only 0.7% in real terms and direct government farm payments rise to $44.3 billion. I translate the large, persistent, mixed agricultural economy and continuing program flows into broadly stable five-year constant-quality regulatory demand. Receipts may overstate workload if digital compliance becomes simpler or understate it if disease, trade, climate, traceability, and emergency programs intensify regulation.
caveats: Farm cash receipts are an economic-activity proxy, not a regulatory-output measure.
One-year forecasts do not capture policy discontinuities.
d5.change_evidence: Agency five-year workload plans covering inspections, licenses, certificates, samples, cases, program participants, imports, outbreaks, and statutory mandates by jurisdiction.
d5.rationale: Agricultural markets, food safety, plant and animal health, grading, market integrity, and farm programs persist, but workload varies with policy, outbreaks, trade, disasters, and program design rather than receipts alone.

caveats: Private contractors may support agencies but are not establishments inside this public-administration code.
e.change_evidence: A separately scoped private inspection, certification, or agricultural consulting industry with a defined firm universe would be necessary and would constitute a different screen.
e.rationale: The statutory population is governmental and the frozen private-firm count is unavailable, so there is no defensible denominator of lower-middle-market eligible firms.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($7,161,744,444) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Reduced workload belongs in d5; internal automation does not remove the accountable regulator.
o.change_evidence: Statutory changes that privatize material regulatory authority or eliminate government program functions rather than merely digitizing them.
o.rationale: Remaining grading, inspection, quarantine, enforcement, program administration, and public accountability require an authorized government operator. AI and third parties can support evidence collection, but software cannot exercise sovereign regulatory authority.

caveats: Agency budget retention and fee-setting rules vary but are not commercial benefit retention.
q.change_evidence: A re-scoped private fee-for-service regulator-support market with contract pricing and renewal data would be necessary.
q.rationale: Appropriated or fee-funded government programs do not retain implemented gross benefits through commercial pricing, renewal repricing, or customer competition as defined by q.

caveats: Automation of administrative preparation does not authorize autonomous enforcement or final grading decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Audited federal and state deployments showing operational coverage, subgroup error, appeals, inspection outcomes, implementation cost, and avoided hours.
rho.rationale: Document intake, records search, risk scoring, scheduling, translation, drafting, and image triage can implement within five years, while procurement, explainability, due process, cybersecurity, data fragmentation, rulemaking, and union constraints limit realization.

caveats: Agency reorganizations, intergovernmental transfers, outsourcing awards, and elected-administration changes are not company control sales.
s5.change_evidence: A re-scoped private vendor population outside 926140 with observed control transactions would be required.
s5.rationale: Government regulatory establishments do not undergo qualifying private-company control transfers, and there is no eligible-firm denominator.

principal_driver: Document-heavy regulatory workflows and expanding agricultural data create substantial supervised AI-assistance opportunities.
principal_weakness: The industry is exclusively governmental, leaving no eligible private-firm acquisition or commercial-retention construct.

run_meta.title: Regulation of Agricultural Marketing and Commodities

sources.supports: Census defines 926140 as government establishments administering agricultural production, marketing, and utilization programs and regulating grading and inspection of food, plants, animals, and other agricultural products.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Regulation of Agricultural Marketing and Commodities

sources.supports: BLS reports 12,660 agricultural inspectors; state, combined-government, and local-government categories employ 4,880, 2,810, and 890 respectively, and duties include compliance inspection for health, quality, and safety.
sources.title: Occupational Employment and Wages, May 2023: Agricultural Inspectors

sources.supports: USDA forecasts $514.7 billion of 2026 farm cash receipts, a 4.5% real decline; crop receipts decline 0.7% in real terms and direct government farm payments reach $44.3 billion.
sources.title: Farm Sector Income and Finances: 2026 Forecast

title: Regulation of Agricultural Marketing and Commodities
